sites.name: Hydrophobic arm
sites.why: PXR's pocket is ~1,150 Å³ and essentially uncharged — 20 of ~28 lining residues are hydrophobic — so this bicyclic ring gets no directional contact to orient it and can sit in any of five near-isoenergetic sub-sites. And AF3-class models are biased to pin polar head-groups while actively mis-placing hydrophobic groups — ligand-orientation-within-pocket is their single biggest failure mode (58.6% of errors) — so the ring landed in the wrong sub-site, 6.6 Å off.
sites.cite: Sci Rep 2018 (uncharged pocket, 5 SR12813 orientations); AF3 docking-failure analysis 2025.

sites.name: Polar anchor
sites.why: Ser247/Gln285/His407 are the polar anchor cluster that pins canonical agonists like SR12813, and Cys284 is part of the same hydrogen-bond hot-spot. But our ligand only offers a thiocarbonyl (C=S) here, and sulfur is a weak, anisotropic H-bond acceptor — ~20% weaker than a carbonyl O and accepting only along specific lone-pair directions. That shallow, directional signal is easy to miss, so it slipped ~4 Å.
sites.cite: Sci Rep 2018 (S247/Q285/H407 anchoring); hot-spot H-bond map; ACS/PMC (C=S weak acceptor).

sites.name: His407 contact
sites.why: His407 is a key directional anchor — it accepts a hydrogen bond from agonist hydroxyls (T0901317, SR12813). Here the ligand only reaches it with a weak edge-on methoxy contact, and His407's side chain is mobile in a pocket that reshapes around each ligand, so the model placed the group ~4.5 Å away.
